title: Loyalty Without Leverage: India’s US Tilt Yields Little
gist: The US has quietly reverted its 'Indo-Pacific Command' to the older 'US Pacific Command' designation, undoing a 2018 first-Trump-administration policy and treating the Indian Ocean as a strategic backup — a downgrade the author frames as India's US tilt yielding little. (Text is a truncated teaser ending in '[...]'.)

insights.claim: The US has quietly reverted its 'Indo-Pacific Command' back to the traditional 'U.S. Pacific Command' (USPACOM) designation, signaling a recalibration of South Asian geopolitics.
insights.speaker: Anuradha Chenoy
insights.so_what: Dropping 'Indo-Pacific' branding hints at reduced US strategic prioritization of India and the Indian Ocean — a shift to watch for India-US defense and trade dynamics.
insights.quote: It has reverted its “Indo-Pacific Command” back to its traditional designation of U.S. Pacific Command (USPACOM).
insights.locator: 'In a move that signaled a blunt recalibration'

insights.claim: The reversal undoes a 2018 first-Trump-administration policy that symbolically merged US maritime interests across the Pacific and Indian oceans, and under the restructured USPACOM the Indian Ocean is being treated largely as a strategic backup plan.
insights.speaker: Anuradha Chenoy
insights.so_what: Demoting the Indian Ocean to backup status implies India gains little leverage from its US alignment — a contrarian read on the India-US partnership trajectory.
insights.quote: Under the newly restructured USPACOM, the Indian Ocean is being treated largely as a strategic back up plan.
